content:
# Leviticus Chapter 13 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 65r–65v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 97–98

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying,

**2** "When a person has on the skin of his body a swelling or a scab or a bright spot, and it becomes on the skin of his body an outbreak of skin disease, he shall be brought to Aaron the priest or to one of his sons the priests.

**3** And the priest shall examine the outbreak on the skin of his body. If the hair in the outbreak has turned white and the outbreak appears to be deeper than the skin of his body, it is an outbreak of skin disease. When the priest has examined him, he shall pronounce him unclean.

**4** But if the bright spot is white on the skin of his body and appears no deeper than the skin, and the hair in it has not turned white, the priest shall shut up the diseased person for seven days.

**5** And the priest shall examine him on the seventh day, and if in his eyes the outbreak is the same and the outbreak has not spread on the skin, then the priest shall shut him up for another seven days.

**6** And the priest shall examine him again on the seventh day, and if the outbreak has faded and the outbreak has not spread on the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him clean; it is a scab. And he shall wash his clothes and be clean.

**7** But if the scab spreads on the skin after he has shown himself to the priest for his cleansing, he shall show himself to the priest again.

**8** And the priest shall examine him, and if the scab has spread on the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is a skin disease.

**9** "When a person has a skin disease, he shall be brought to the priest.

**10** And the priest shall examine him, and if there is a white swelling on the skin that has turned the hair white, and there is raw flesh in the swelling,

**11** it is a chronic skin disease on the skin of his body, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean. He shall not shut him up, for he is unclean.

**12** And if the skin disease breaks out widely on the skin, and the skin disease covers all the skin of the diseased person from head to foot, so far as the priest can see,

**13** then the priest shall look, and if the skin disease has covered all his body, he shall pronounce him clean of the outbreak. It has all turned white; he is clean.

**14** But on the day raw flesh appears on him, he shall be unclean.

**15** And the priest shall examine the raw flesh and pronounce him unclean. The raw flesh is unclean; it is a skin disease.

**16** But if the raw flesh turns again and is changed to white, he shall come to the priest,

**17** and the priest shall examine him, and if the outbreak has turned white, then the priest shall pronounce the diseased person clean; he is clean.

**18** "And when there is on the skin of one's body a boil and it heals,

**19** and in the place of the boil there is a white swelling or a reddish-white bright spot, he shall show himself to the priest.

**20** And the priest shall look, and if it appears deeper than the skin and its hair has turned white, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean. It is an outbreak of skin disease that has broken out in the boil.

**21** But if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in it and it is not deeper than the skin but has faded, then the priest shall shut him up seven days.

**22** And if it spreads on the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is a disease.

**23** But if the bright spot remains in its place and does not spread, it is the scar of the boil, and the priest shall pronounce him clean.

**24** "Or when the body has a burn on its skin and the raw flesh of the burn becomes a reddish-white or white bright spot,

**25** then the priest shall examine it, and if the hair in the bright spot has turned white and it appears deeper than the skin, it is a skin disease that has broken out in the burn, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is an outbreak of skin disease.

**26** But if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in the bright spot and it is no deeper than the skin but has faded, then the priest shall shut him up seven days.

**27** And the priest shall examine him on the seventh day. If it has spread on the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is an outbreak of skin disease.

**28** But if the bright spot remains in its place and has not spread on the skin but has faded, it is a swelling from the burn, and the priest shall pronounce him clean, for it is a scar of the burn.

**29** "When a man or woman has a disease on the head or the beard,

**30** the priest shall examine the disease. And if it appears deeper than the skin and the hair in it is yellow and thin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean. It is an itch, a skin disease of the head or the beard.

**31** And if the priest examines the itching disease and it appears no deeper than the skin and there is no black hair in it, then the priest shall shut up the person with the itching disease for seven days.

**32** And on the seventh day the priest shall examine the disease. If the itch has not spread and there is no yellow hair in it and the itch appears no deeper than the skin,

**33** then he shall shave himself, but the itch he shall not shave. And the priest shall shut up the person with the itch for another seven days.

**34** And on the seventh day the priest shall examine the itch, and if the itch has not spread on the skin and it appears no deeper than the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him clean. And he shall wash his clothes and be clean.

**35** But if the itch spreads on the skin after his cleansing,

**36** then the priest shall examine him, and if the itch has spread on the skin, the priest need not seek for the yellow hair; he is unclean.

**37** But if in his eyes the itch is unchanged and black hair has grown in it, the itch has healed. He is clean, and the priest shall pronounce him clean.

**38** "When a man or a woman has bright spots on the skin of the body — white bright spots —

**39** the priest shall look, and if the bright spots on the skin of the body are of a dull white, it is a rash that has broken out on the skin; he is clean.

**40** "And if a man's hair falls out from his head, he is bald; he is clean.

**41** And if a man's hair falls out from his forehead, he has baldness of the forehead; he is clean.

**42** But if there is on the bald head or the bald forehead a reddish-white disease, it is a skin disease breaking out on his bald head or his bald forehead.

**43** Then the priest shall examine him, and if the swelling of the disease on his bald head or bald forehead is reddish-white, like the appearance of skin disease in the skin of the body,

**44** he is a diseased person, he is unclean. The priest shall pronounce him unclean; his disease is on his head.

**45** "The diseased person who has the disease shall wear torn clothes and let the hair of his head hang loose, and he shall cover his upper lip and cry out, 'Unclean, unclean!'

**46** He shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease. He is unclean. He shall dwell alone. His dwelling shall be outside the camp.

**47** "As for a garment, when a case of skin disease is in it — in a woolen garment or a linen garment,

**48** whether in the warp or the woof of linen or wool, or in a skin or in anything made of skin —

**49** if the disease is greenish or reddish in the garment or in the skin or in the warp or the woof or in any article made of skin, it is an outbreak of skin disease, and it shall be shown to the priest.

**50** And the priest shall examine the disease and shut up that which has the disease for seven days.

**51** Then he shall examine the disease on the seventh day. If the disease has spread in the garment, in the warp or the woof, or in the skin, for whatever use the skin is employed, the disease is a persistent skin disease; it is unclean.

**52** He shall burn the garment, or the warp or the woof, the wool or the linen, or any article of skin that is diseased, for it is a persistent skin disease. It shall be burned in fire.

**53** "And if the priest examines, and the disease has not spread in the garment, in the warp or the woof, or in any article of skin,

**54** then the priest shall command that they wash the thing in which is the disease, and he shall shut it up for another seven days.

**55** And the priest shall examine the diseased thing after it has been washed. And if the diseased spot has not changed in color, though the disease has not spread, it is unclean. You shall burn it in fire, whether the rot is on the back or on the front.

**56** "But if the priest examines, and the disease has faded after it has been washed, he shall tear it out of the garment or the skin or the warp or the woof.

**57** Then if it appears again in the garment, in the warp or the woof, or in any article of skin, it is spreading. You shall burn with fire that in which is the disease.

**58** But the garment, or the warp or the woof, or any article of skin from which the disease departs when you have washed it, shall then be washed a second time, and it shall be clean.

**59** This is the law for a case of skin disease in a garment of wool or linen, either in the warp or the woof, or in any article of skin, for pronouncing it clean or unclean."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 65r–65v.** Leviticus 13 is the longest chapter in the book of Leviticus, containing 59 verses, and it dominates both manuscript folios fully. Folio 65r begins with the opening diagnostic laws (vv. 1 ff.) following the brief chapter 12 (which closes in the opening lines of the folio); folio 65v carries the continuation of the chapter through the garment-disease regulations at the end of v. 59. The opening parashah break from chapter 12 to chapter 13 is marked in the manuscript with a setumah (closed section) indicator, though the larger block of purity legislation continues unbroken in its priestly-legislative genre: both chapters deal with conditions that generate ritual impurity, chapter 12 through childbirth and chapter 13 through tzara'at. The chapter is structured by a series of conditional-case legal units, each following the characteristic priestly casuistic form: "when a person has X condition, the priest shall examine it; if Y is observed, he shall pronounce him unclean; if Z is observed, he shall shut him up seven days" — an iterative procedural logic that repeats through nine distinct diagnostic scenarios. The Masoretic accentuation is particularly careful throughout this chapter, with extensive use of the pashta, zaqef qatan, and revia accent marks to define the rhythmic cadences of the legal clauses. The Tiberian vocalization system's careful work is evident in the fine distinctions among the technical skin-condition terms: se'et (swelling), sapachat (scab), baheret (bright spot), and negah tzara'at (outbreak of skin disease) are all precisely pointed. The manuscript shows the characteristic three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex, with the Masoretic apparatus in the margins. Several parashiyot (section breaks) appear within the chapter at the major diagnostic sub-units (boil, burn, head/beard, bright spots, baldness, garments), reflecting the editors' recognition of the chapter's internal structure.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 97–98 (LXX).** The LXX text of Leviticus 13 on Vaticanus pages 97–98 is extensive, given the chapter's length, and the two pages are densely written in the Vaticanus uncial hand. Page 97 carries the tail of Leviticus 12 (visible in the upper section of the page) followed by the beginning of chapter 13; the chapter identification καπ. λζ or similar notation for chapter numbering may be visible in the margin. Page 98 carries the continuation of chapter 13 through the garment-disease laws and concludes at v. 59. The LXX's rendering of this chapter shows consistent Greek translation conventions for the technical skin-condition vocabulary: ἁφή ("touch, outbreak") renders negah; λέπρα ("skin disease") renders tzara'at throughout; and the diagnostic procedural verbs — ὄψεται ὁ ἱερεύς ("the priest shall look/examine"), ἀκάθαρτος αὐτόν κρινεῖ ("he shall judge/pronounce him unclean"), καθαριεῖ αὐτόν ("he shall pronounce him clean") — are used systematically. The LXX translator rendered this chapter with particular attention to procedural consistency, maintaining the casuistic legal structure and the iterative conditional logic of the MT. A number of significant divergences exist, particularly in the diagnostic terminology (the LXX consistently uses λέπρα, which later translators rendered "leprosy," for tzara'at, though neither term accurately describes Hansen's disease in this context) and in the garment-disease section (vv. 47–59), where the LXX handles the textile terminology with some variation from the MT.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Skin disease" for tzara'at / צָרַעַת (tzara'at) throughout.** The term צָרַעַת (tzara'at) is the most theologically and medically significant term in this chapter. It is traditionally rendered "leprosy" in the KJV and older translations, following the LXX's λέπρα and Vulgate's lepra. However, tzara'at does not designate Hansen's disease (true leprosy, Mycobacterium leprae), which was almost certainly unknown in the ancient Near East during the period of the Pentateuch's composition, and whose clinical presentation does not match the diagnostic criteria described in Leviticus 13. Modern scholarship (Milgrom, Wenham, Hartley, HALOT) is unanimous that tzara'at is a ritual-diagnostic category covering a range of skin conditions: fungal infections, psoriasis, vitiligo, eczema, various dermatoses, and possibly other conditions that produce the relevant visual signs (whitening, spreading lesions, raw flesh, shiny or depressed appearance). The priest's role is not that of a physician treating a pathological condition but of a ritual diagnostician determining the purity-status of the affected person. The word tzara'at appears to derive from a root meaning "to strike" (cf. Arabic sara'a) and may carry the connotation of being "struck" with affliction — in later biblical usage (Num 12:10; 2 Sam 3:29; 2 Kgs 5:27) tzara'at is a divine judgment or affliction. Throughout this translation, tzara'at is consistently rendered "skin disease" (following ESV, NASB, NIV modern editions) with the recognition that this is a Levitical ritual-diagnostic category. The LXX λέπρα is NOT rendered "leprosy" in this translation; in the LXX variant table the Greek term is noted. The technical term is preserved in the translation notes where the Hebrew appears.

2. **"Outbreak of skin disease" for negah tzara'at / נֶגַע צָרַעַת (negah tzara'at, vv. 2–3 etc.).** Hebrew נֶגַע (negah — "stroke, blow, plague, outbreak, mark, infection") is a broader term than tzara'at itself: negah can refer to any "striking" or affliction — it is used of the plagues of Egypt (Exod 11:1), of physical blows (Deut 17:8; 21:5), and throughout Leviticus 13–14 as the technical term for the skin "outbreak" or "mark" that is subject to priestly examination. When combined with tzara'at, negah tzara'at means "an outbreak/infection of skin disease." "Outbreak of skin disease" renders negah tzara'at with formal precision; "outbreak" preserves the connotation of something that has erupted or broken out, consistent with the visual presentation of spreading lesions. The LXX renders negah consistently as ἁφή ("touch, contact, outbreak") — an unusual choice that may reflect the idea of the skin condition as a "touching" or "contacting" of the body by the affliction. Alternatively, ἁφή may derive from the skin literally being touched or affected by the condition.

3. **"Swelling," "scab," and "bright spot" for se'et, sapachat, baheret / שְׂאֵת, סַפַּחַת, בַּהֶרֶת (se'et, sapachat, baheret, v. 2).** These three terms in v. 2 appear to name the three primary visual presentations of potential tzara'at that prompt priestly examination: (a) שְׂאֵת (se'et — from נָשָׂא, nasa', "to lift, to raise") — a "raising, elevation, swelling" of the skin surface; (b) סַפַּחַת (sapachat — from סָפַח, safach, "to attach, to join, to append, to be added") — a "scab, rash, eruption" that attaches to or spreads across the skin; the verb safach also means "to pour out" and "to add" — the sapachat is something appended to the skin surface; (c) בַּהֶרֶת (baheret — from בָּהַר, bahar, "to be bright, to shine, to be clear") — a "bright spot, shining mark, glistening patch" on the skin. The three terms form a clinical triad that covers the range of potentially problematic skin presentations: raised swellings, scabby eruptions, and bright/white spots. Later in the chapter (vv. 19, 24, 28) se'et and baheret appear again in specific diagnostic contexts. "Swelling," "scab," and "bright spot" render se'et, sapachat, and baheret respectively, following the established renderings of ESV and NASB. The LXX renders the triad as οὐλή ("scar, mark"), σημασία/σημεῖον ("sign, mark"), and τηλαύγημα ("brightness, gleaming spot") — showing the Greek translator's attempt to find Greek equivalents for the three skin-presentation categories.

4. **"The priest shall examine" for ve-ra'ah ha-kohen / וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן (ve-ra'ah ha-kohen) throughout.** The verb רָאָה (ra'ah — "to see, to look, to examine, to inspect") in the qal perfect third-person singular ("and the priest saw/looked/examined") functions as the procedural opening verb in virtually every diagnostic unit throughout the chapter. The priest's role throughout chapter 13 is consistently described by ra'ah — he "looks at" or "examines" the condition. This is not a specialist medical examination in the modern sense but a visual inspection against specific criteria (whitening of hair, depth, spreading, etc.). The priestly function here is quasi-judicial: the priest does not treat or diagnose the cause but pronounces a verdict (unclean/clean) based on observable visual criteria. "The priest shall examine" renders ve-ra'ah ha-kohen precisely (ESV, NASB: "the priest shall examine"). The LXX renders consistently with ὄψεται ὁ ἱερεύς ("the priest shall see/look"), using the future of ὁράω ("to see").

5. **"He shall pronounce him unclean" / "he shall pronounce him clean" for ve-timme'o ha-kohen / ve-tihharo ha-kohen (vv. 3, 6, 8, etc.).** The verbs וְטִמְּאוֹ (ve-timme'o — piel perfect of טָמֵא, tameh, "to be unclean"; piel: "to declare/pronounce unclean") and וְטִהֲרוֹ (ve-tihharo — piel of טָהֵר, taher, "to be clean"; piel: "to declare/pronounce clean") are the two judicial verdict-formulae of the priestly diagnostic procedure. Both are piel (causative/declarative) forms: the priest does not make the person unclean or clean but pronounces, declares, or adjudicates their status as unclean or clean. This declarative/performative force of the piel is essential: the priest's pronouncement does not cause the condition but officially establishes the ritual status. "Pronounce him unclean" / "pronounce him clean" renders the piel declarative force (ESV, NASB use the same rendering). The LXX renders both with κρινεῖ αὐτόν ("he shall judge/adjudicate for him") — using κρίνω ("to judge, to decide, to adjudicate") for the priestly verdict, which captures the judicial function well. The LXX also uses ἀκαθαρτίζω for the "pronounce unclean" formula and καθαρίζω for "pronounce clean."

6. **"He shall shut him up" for ve-hisgir ha-kohen / וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן (ve-hisgir ha-kohen, vv. 4, 5, 11, 21, 26, 31, 33, 50, 54).** The verb הִסְגִּיר (hisgir — hifil of סָגַר, sagar, "to close, to shut, to lock") is the technical term for the seven-day quarantine or isolation procedure imposed by the priest when the condition is ambiguous — neither clearly unclean nor clearly clean. The hifil causative form means "to cause to be shut up" or "to impose confinement upon." The person under examination is "shut up" — confined, isolated, held in an indeterminate state — pending re-examination. This is not an imprisonment but a controlled isolation for the purpose of diagnostic observation: the condition is monitored over seven days to see whether it spreads (indicating disease) or fades (indicating a benign condition). The verb sagar also appears in contexts of shutting doors, closing locks, delivering someone into enemy hands (Deut 23:16), and closing up a wound (Gen 2:21 — "he closed up the flesh"). "He shall shut him up" renders ve-hisgir ha-kohen precisely, preserving the sense of enforced confinement (ESV, NASB: "the priest shall shut him up"). The LXX renders consistently with ἀφοριεῖ αὐτόν ("he shall set him apart/isolate him") — using ἀφορίζω ("to set apart, to separate, to segregate") for hisgir — a term that captures the isolation dimension without the "locking in" connotation of sagar.

7. **"Chronic skin disease" / "persistent skin disease" for tzara'at noshenet / tzara'at memeret (vv. 11, 51–52).** Two modifiers on tzara'at appear in the chapter: (a) צָרַעַת נוֹשֶׁנֶת (tzara'at noshenet — "a long-standing, chronic, old skin disease") in v. 11: נוֹשֶׁנֶת is the qal feminine participle of יָשַׁן (yashan — "to be old, to grow old, to be long-standing"); (b) צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת (tzara'at mameret — "a persistent, malignant, fretful skin disease") in vv. 51–52: מַמְאֶרֶת is the hifil feminine participle of מָאַר (ma'ar — "to pain, to be sore, to be malignant" — a root of uncertain derivation, possibly related to the sense of festering or causing pain); the LXX renders it with ἐντετηκυῖα ("embedded, deeply ingrained"). "Chronic skin disease" renders tzara'at noshenet in v. 11 (the condition is long-established, not newly appeared); "persistent skin disease" renders tzara'at mameret in vv. 51–52 (the condition in the garment is recurrently spreading, it returns after washing). The distinction is preserved in the translation. The LXX renders noshenet with παλαιοῦσθαι ("to become old, to be of long standing") in v. 11 and mameret with the noted ἐντετηκυῖα in vv. 51–52.

8. **"Raw flesh" for basar chai / בָּשָׂר חַי (basar chai, vv. 10, 14–16, 24).** Hebrew בָּשָׂר חַי (basar chai — "living flesh, living skin, raw flesh") is the diagnostic sign of active, aggressive skin disease. בָּשָׂר (basar — "flesh, body, skin") is combined with חַי (chai — "living, alive, raw") — the "living flesh" that is visible in or beneath the lesion indicates that the disease is active and the skin is in a state of open, unhealed tissue. Raw flesh appearing in a swelling (v. 10) or in a burn (v. 24) is an indicator of unclean status; conversely, the withdrawal of raw flesh (v. 16) as the condition changes to white is a positive diagnostic sign. "Raw flesh" renders basar chai precisely — "raw" captures the sense of exposed, unhealed, living tissue, as opposed to the dry, scaled, or whitened skin of resolved conditions. The LXX renders basar chai as σὰρξ ζῶσα ("living flesh") — literal agreement with the Hebrew.

9. **"Itching disease" / "itch" for neteq / נֶתֶק (neteq, vv. 30–37).** Hebrew נֶתֶק (neteq — from נָתַק, natak, "to pull apart, to detach, to break") is the specific term for the head/beard skin condition described in vv. 29–37. The term appears to designate a condition affecting the scalp or face (head or beard region) — possibly tinea capitis (ringworm of the scalp) or similar fungal infection causing hair loss, characterized by yellow-colored, thin hair growth within the lesion. The diagnostic markers for neteq differ from the standard tzara'at criteria: yellow thin hair (rather than white) and depth below the skin level are the key indicators of unclean neteq. "Itching disease" / "itch" renders neteq in this context, following the ESV rendering; NASB uses "scale" or "scall"; NEB uses "ringworm." The term is a technical diagnostic category that does not have a modern medical equivalent. The LXX renders neteq as θραῦσμα ("fracture, break, splitting") — reflecting the sense of natak ("to break apart, to detach"), applied to the splitting or breaking apart of the scalp skin.

10. **"Rash" for bohaq / בֹּהַק (bohaq, v. 39).** Hebrew בֹּהַק (bohaq) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its etymology connects it to the root בהק which relates to brightness or shininess. It appears to designate a benign skin condition — a "rash" or "tetter" (KJV: "freckled spot") that produces white spots similar in appearance to baheret ("bright spot") but dull white rather than brilliant white, and not a marker of genuine tzara'at. The diagnostic distinguishing feature between baheret and bohaq is that bohaq is "of dull white" (or "whitish/dim") — not the glistening white that characterizes pathological tzara'at. The person with bohaq is declared clean. "Rash" renders bohaq as the most accessible modern equivalent for a benign, non-disqualifying skin discoloration. The LXX renders it as τηλαύγημα ("brightness from afar") — using the same term as for baheret, which may reflect the translator's difficulty in distinguishing the two terms.

11. **"Cover his upper lip" for ve-al safam ya'teh / וְעַל-שָׂפָם יַעְטֶה (ve-al safam ya'teh, v. 45).** Hebrew שָׂפָם (safam — "mustache, upper lip, beard area below the nose") combined with יַעְטֶה (ya'teh — qal imperfect of עָטָה, 'atah, "to wrap, to cover, to envelop") describes the ritual gesture of the diagnosed-unclean person: covering the lower part of the face at the upper lip / mustache area. This gesture is attested in Ezek 24:17, 22 as a mourning gesture (the mourner covers his mouth/upper lip). In Leviticus 13:45, the gesture serves two simultaneous functions: (1) it is a visible bodily sign of the person's state of ritual impurity / mourning-like exclusion, marking them publicly as one who is under a social and ritual restriction; (2) it physically marks the mouth, preventing the speaking of blessing, blessing at meals, or ordinary social greeting — the covered mouth signals social and ritual withdrawal. "Cover his upper lip" renders ve-al safam ya'teh precisely (ESV: "cover his upper lip"; NASB: "cover his mustache"). The LXX renders περιβαλεῖται τὸ ἱμάτιον αὐτοῦ ("he shall wrap his garment around") — diverging significantly from the MT: the LXX has the person wrapping their garment around them rather than covering their upper lip. This is either a textual variant, a misreading of the Hebrew, or a deliberate interpretive choice by the LXX translator.

12. **"Unclean, unclean!" for tameh tameh yiqra / טָמֵא טָמֵא יִקְרָא (tameh tameh yiqra, v. 45).** The double cry — tameh tameh — is a unique formulation in the Hebrew Bible. The adjective טָמֵא (tameh — "unclean") is repeated twice without a conjunction: not "unclean and unclean" but "Unclean! Unclean!" — a doubled exclamation functioning as a public announcement and warning. The cry serves as an audible warning to others to avoid contact with the afflicted person, preventing inadvertent contagion of impurity. The repetition may also reflect intensity — a doubled exclamatory announcement rather than simple information. The verb יִקְרָא (yiqra — qal imperfect of קָרָא, qara', "to call, to cry out, to proclaim") means "he shall cry out / he shall call out" — the afflicted person is required to make this public proclamation himself whenever he is in the proximity of others. "Unclean, unclean!" renders tameh tameh yiqra precisely, capturing the doubled exclamation. The LXX renders the cry as ἀκάθαρτος ἀκάθαρτος καλεῖται ("he is called 'unclean, unclean'") — rendering the doubled tameh with doubled ἀκάθαρτος, in close agreement with the doubling effect of the MT.

13. **"He shall dwell alone" for badad yeshev / בָּדָד יֵשֵׁב (badad yeshev, v. 46).** Hebrew בָּדָד (badad — "alone, in isolation, apart") combined with יֵשֵׁב (yeshev — qal imperfect of יָשַׁב, yashav, "to sit, to dwell, to inhabit") describes the social isolation required of the person declared unclean with tzara'at: "he shall dwell in isolation / he shall live alone." The word badad appears in Lam 1:1 — "How she sits alone (badad yeshevah ha-'ir)" — describing Jerusalem sitting in desolation. The connection is significant: the experience of the tzara'at-afflicted person is a form of living desolation, an exclusion from community life. "He shall dwell alone" renders badad yeshev precisely (ESV, NASB). The LXX renders μόνος κατοικήσει ("he shall dwell alone") — using μόνος ("alone, only") for badad and κατοικέω ("to dwell, to inhabit") for yashav — in close agreement.

14. **"Warp" and "woof" for sheti and erev / שְׁתִי וְעֵרֶב (sheti ve-erev, vv. 48–59).** These two terms designate the fundamental components of woven textile structure: (a) שְׁתִי (sheti — "warp," the longitudinal threads set up on the loom before weaving) — possibly from a root meaning "to set, to establish, to lay" — the foundational threads running lengthwise in the fabric; (b) עֵרֶב ('erev — "woof, weft," the transverse threads woven across the warp on the loom) — from the root עָרַב ('arav, "to mix, to interweave, to intermingle") — the cross-threaded elements interwoven through the warp. Together sheti ve-erev designate the whole structure of a woven fabric: the skin disease in a garment can affect either the lengthwise fibers (sheti) or the cross-fibers ('erev) or both. "Warp" and "woof" preserve the traditional technical textile terminology (ESV, KJV: "warp or woof"; NASB: "warp or woof"). The LXX renders the two terms as στήμων ("warp thread, the set-up threads") and κρόκη ("woof, the woven cross-thread") — using the standard Greek technical terms for loom-components, in close agreement with the MT.

15. **"Greenish or reddish" for yeraqraq o adamdam / יְרַקְרַק אוֹ אֲדַמְדַּם (yeraqraq o adamdam, v. 49).** These two color terms are unusual in the Hebrew Bible: (a) יְרַקְרַק (yeraqraq — intensive/doubling form of יָרֹק, yaroq, "green, yellow-green") — "greenish, yellowish-green" — the doubling of the root-consonants (yaraq-yaraq → yeraqraq) intensifies the color-quality, suggesting a vivid or deep greenish hue; (b) אֲדַמְדַּם (adamdam — intensive/doubling form of אָדֹם, adom, "red") — "reddish, red-tinged" — the same doubling intensification pattern as yeraqraq. Both intensified color-forms appear exclusively in Leviticus 13–14 as diagnostic color indicators for tzara'at in garments (13:49) and in house-stones (14:37). "Greenish or reddish" renders yeraqraq o adamdam with standard formal equivalence (ESV, NASB: "greenish or reddish"). The LXX renders χλωρίζον ἢ πυρρίζον ("greenish or reddish/flame-colored") — using χλωρός ("green/yellow-green") and πυρρός ("flame-colored, fiery-red, reddish") — in close functional agreement.

16. **"The rot is on the back or on the front" for she-ha-kechem be-qarchato o be-gabbe'to / אִם-בְּקָרַחְתּוֹ אִם-בְּגַבַּחְתּוֹ (im-be-qarchato im-be-gabbeto, v. 55).** Hebrew קָרַחַת (qarachat) and גַּבַּחַת (gabbachat) appear in chapter 13 first in the baldness section (vv. 40–44) as technical terms for baldness at the crown of the head (qarachat) and baldness at the forehead (gabbachat). Here in v. 55, in the garment section, the same two terms are repurposed as a metaphor for the two sides of a fabric: the "bald back" (qarachat) and the "bald front" (gabbachat) of a cloth — that is, the back and front face of the textile, both subject to examination. This reuse of the baldness terminology in the garment section is a deliberate literary device, extending the bodily-condition vocabulary to material conditions: the cloth, like a human body, has surfaces that can be afflicted. "Whether the rot is on the back or on the front" renders the clause im-be-qarchato im-be-gabbeto idiomatically, following ESV ("whether the rot is on the back or on the front") and NASB ("whether on the front or back"). The LXX renders ἐάν τε ἐν τῷ γυμνώματι αὐτοῦ ἐάν τε ἐν τῷ τριχώματι αὐτοῦ ("whether in its bare area or in its hairy area") — apparently reading the textile metaphor in terms of the cloth's nap or texture rather than front/back, and retaining the body-surface terminology differently.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 97–98) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13:2 | tzara'at ("skin disease" — ritual diagnostic category) | λέπρα ("leprosy/skin disease") | LXX consistently renders tzara'at as λέπρα throughout the chapter. λέπρα in classical Greek designated various scaly skin conditions (from λεπρός, "scaly, rough"), not Hansen's disease specifically; however, the LXX's choice of λέπρα became the vector through which "leprosy" entered the Western translation tradition. The MT's tzara'at is a broader ritual-diagnostic category; the LXX's λέπρα narrowed the term toward a specific skin presentation (scaliness). |
| 13:2 | se'et, sapachat, baheret ("swelling, scab, bright spot" — three lesion types) | οὐλή, σημασία, τηλαύγημα ("scar/mark, sign/mark, brightness/gleam") | LXX renders the three diagnostic terms with three different Greek words: οὐλή (scar/mark) for se'et, σημασία or σημεῖον (sign, mark) for sapachat, and τηλαύγημα (brightness, gleaming) for baheret. The Greek terms approximate the Hebrew but do not correspond precisely. Notably the LXX renders sapachat with a term meaning "sign" rather than "scab/eruption," which loses the sense of spreading/adhering implied by the root sfch. |
| 13:3 | u-marah ha-nega amok me-'or besaro ("the outbreak appears deeper than the skin of his body") | καὶ ἡ ὄψις αὐτοῦ ταπεινοτέρα τοῦ δέρματος τοῦ χρωτός ("and its appearance is lower/deeper than the skin of the body") | LXX renders amok ("deep") with ταπεινός ("low, humble, sunken") — a rendering that captures the visual appearance of a lesion that is depressed or sunken below the skin surface, which is perhaps more precise visually than the abstract "deeper." Close agreement; minor nuance. |
| 13:4 | hisgir ha-kohen et ha-nega shivat yamim ("the priest shall shut up the outbreak/person for seven days") | ἀφοριεῖ αὐτόν ὁ ἱερεύς ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("the priest shall set him apart for seven days") | LXX uses ἀφορίζω ("to set apart, to segregate, to separate") for hisgir ("to shut up, to confine"). ἀφορίζω is the technical verb for separation in Jewish practice (cf. the synagogue practice of aph orism os, excommunication). The MT's sagar conveys physical confinement; the LXX's aphorizō conveys social/ritual separation. The practical outcome is similar but the conceptual frame differs. |
| 13:11 | tzara'at noshenet hi ("it is a chronic skin disease") | λέπρα ἐνιαυσίζουσα ("a year-long / perennial leprosy") | LXX renders noshenet ("old, long-standing," qal ptc. of yashan) as ἐνιαυσίζω ("to be yearly, to go on year after year") — interpreting the "old" as "of many years' standing, annual/perennial." The MT's noshenet emphasizes the antiquity/persistence of the condition; the LXX's eniausizousa emphasizes its year-long duration. A meaningful interpretive shift: the LXX implies a precisely time-bounded chronic condition. |
| 13:12 | ve-im paroa tifrace ha-tzara'at be-'or ("if the skin disease breaks out widely on the skin") | ἐὰν δὲ ἐξανθήσασα ἐξανθήσῃ ἡ λέπρα ("but if the leprosy breaks into flower abundantly") | LXX renders paroa tifrace ("if it should break out widely/fully") with ἐξανθέω ("to break out into flower, to bloom, to erupt abundantly") — using a verb of flowering/blooming to describe the skin disease erupting across the whole body. This is a striking Greek literary choice: the disease "blooms" or "flowers" over the body. The MT uses the simple verb para ("to break out/loose") + parach ("to sprout, to spread"). The LXX's floral imagery is distinctive. |
| 13:30 | v'einav ayin bo se'ar shahor ("and there is no black hair in it") | καὶ ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν θρὶξ πυρρά ("and in it there is no reddish hair") | In v. 30 the LXX appears to read the hair color as πυρρά (reddish/fiery) rather than μέλαινα (black) — but the standard MT has "black hair" (se'ar shahor) as the diagnostic indicator that the condition is not neteq-related. A possible textual variant: some LXX MSS read differently. The LXX (Vaticanus) may reflect a variant Hebrew Vorlage or a translator's interpretive choice. |
| 13:33 | ve-et ha-neteq lo yegalach ("but the itch he shall not shave") | τὸ δὲ θραῦσμα οὐ ξυρηθήσεται ("but the 'fracture' shall not be shaved") | LXX renders neteq as θραῦσμα (fracture/break) — consistently rendering the term from its etymological root (natak, to break/tear apart) rather than from its clinical presentation. The MT's instruction that the priest shave everything around the neteq but not the neteq itself (to preserve the diagnostic hair evidence within the lesion) is rendered accurately in both MT and LXX. |
| 13:39 | bohaq hu tahor ("it is bohaq, he is clean") | τηλαύγημά ἐστιν ἀνθεῖ τῷ δέρματι αὐτοῦ ("it is a brightness blooming on his skin, he is clean") | LXX renders bohaq as τηλαύγημα ("brightness from afar, gleaming") and adds ἀνθεῖ τῷ δέρματι αὐτοῦ ("blooming on his skin") — an addition not in the MT of this verse. The LXX adds a descriptive phrase about the condition "blooming" on the skin, using the same floral verb (ἀνθέω) as in v. 12. An interpretive LXX expansion. |
| 13:45 | ve-al safam ya'teh ("and he shall cover his upper lip") | καὶ τὸ ἱμάτιον αὐτοῦ ἐσταλμένον ἔσται ("and his garment shall be wrapped/arranged") | LXX diverges significantly from MT in v. 45: where MT has the diseased person "covering his upper lip" (safam ya'teh), LXX has his "garment being wrapped/arranged" (ἱμάτιον ἐσταλμένον). The LXX may have misread עַל-שָׂפָם (al safam, "upon the upper lip/mustache") as a reference to garment-wrapping, or may have had a different Hebrew Vorlage, or the translator may have assimilated the verse to the torn-clothes requirement at the beginning of v. 45. This is a significant divergence affecting the content of the diseased person's ritual behavior. |
| 13:45 | tameh tameh yiqra ("'Unclean, unclean!' he shall cry out") | ἀκάθαρτος ἀκάθαρτος καλεῖται ("he is called 'unclean, unclean'") | LXX preserves the doubled ἀκάθαρτος ἀκάθαρτος for tameh tameh — close agreement on the doubling. However, LXX uses καλεῖται ("he is called," passive) rather than the active proclamation קָרָא ("he shall call/cry out") of the MT. The MT has the diseased person actively proclaiming his impurity; the LXX may be construing this as a passive description of how he is designated. A minor but meaningful shift in the agency of the proclamation. |
| 13:46 | kol yemei asher ha-nega vo yitmah tameh hu ("all the days that the outbreak is on him, he shall be unclean, he is unclean") | πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας ὅσας ἂν ἔχῃ τὴν ἁφήν ἀκάθαρτος ὢν ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ("all the days in which he has the outbreak, being unclean, he shall be unclean") | LXX agrees closely with MT in the declaratory formula for the duration of uncleanness; the LXX adds the participial ἀκάθαρτος ὢν ("being unclean") as a descriptive supplement to the main declaration ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ("he shall be unclean"), making the status continuous and participial rather than simply declarative. Close agreement overall. |
| 13:51 | tzara'at mameret hi ("it is a persistent skin disease") | λέπρα ἐντετηκυῖά ἐστιν ("it is a deep-ingrained / embedded leprosy") | LXX renders mameret ("persistent, malignant" — hifil ptc. of ma'ar) as ἐντετηκυῖα ("having melted into, deeply embedded, ingrained") — using the perfect participle of ἐντήκω ("to melt into, to be fused with, to be deeply set"). The image is of the disease being fused or melted into the fabric of the garment, deeply embedded. The MT's mameret implies recurrence and malignancy; the LXX's entetekyia implies depth of penetration into the material. The two images are related but distinct. |
| 13:55 | be-qarchato o be-gabbeto ("on its back or on its front" — of the garment) | ἐν τῷ γυμνώματι ἢ ἐν τῷ τριχώματι ("in its bare area or in its hairy area") | LXX renders qarachat (baldness of crown) and gabbachat (baldness of forehead), when applied to garments in v. 55, as gymnōma ("bare/naked area") and trichōma ("hairy area") — apparently reading the terms as referring to the nap or texture of the cloth (bare areas vs. pile/hairy areas) rather than front/back surfaces. The MT's reuse of the baldness vocabulary as a textile metaphor is interpreted differently in the LXX. |
| 13:59 | zo't torat nega ha-tzara'at be-veged ("this is the law for a case of skin disease in a garment") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς ἁφῆς τῆς λέπρας ἱματίου ("this is the law of the outbreak of leprosy in a garment") | Close agreement on the concluding torah-formula. LXX uses ἁφή (outbreak) for nega (outbreak/infection) and λέπρα for tzara'at — consistent with the chapter's vocabulary. The summary closing formula in both MT and LXX marks the end of the garment-disease legislative unit and corresponds to the broader pattern of zo't torah ("this is the law") summary formulae throughout Leviticus 11–15. |

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 13)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| יהוה (YHWH) | the LORD | Consistent throughout |
| טָמֵא (tameh) | unclean | Ritual impurity status; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος |
| טָהוֹר (tahor) | clean | Ritual purity status; LXX: καθαρός |
| צָרַעַת (tzara'at) | skin disease | NOT leprosy; ritual diagnostic category; LXX: λέπρα |
| נֶגַע (negah) | outbreak | Skin "strike/blow/infection"; LXX: ἁφή |
| נֶגַע צָרַעַת (negah tzara'at) | outbreak of skin disease | Combined diagnostic term; LXX: ἁφή τῆς λέπρας |
| שְׂאֵת (se'et) | swelling | Raised lesion; LXX: οὐλή |
| סַפַּחַת (sapachat) | scab | Spreading eruption; LXX: σημασία / σημεῖον |
| בַּהֶרֶת (baheret) | bright spot | White glistening mark; LXX: τηλαύγημα |
| בָּשָׂר חַי (basar chai) | raw flesh | Living/exposed tissue; LXX: σὰρξ ζῶσα |
| הִסְגִּיר (hisgir, hifil) | shut up / isolate | Seven-day quarantine procedure; LXX: ἀφορίζω |
| וְטִמְּאוֹ (ve-timme'o, piel) | pronounce him unclean | Declarative piel; LXX: κρινεῖ ἀκάθαρτον |
| וְטִהֲרוֹ (ve-tihharo, piel) | pronounce him clean | Declarative piel; LXX: καθαριεῖ / κρινεῖ καθαρόν |
| וְרָאָה הַכֹּהֵן (ve-ra'ah ha-kohen) | the priest shall examine | Visual inspection formula; LXX: ὄψεται ὁ ἱερεύς |
| נֶתֶק (neteq) | itching disease / itch | Head/beard condition; LXX: θραῦσμα |
| בֹּהַק (bohaq) | rash | Benign dull-white spots; LXX: τηλαύγημα |
| שָׂפָם (safam) | upper lip | Mustache/upper-lip area; LXX diverges (garment) |
| בָּדָד יֵשֵׁב (badad yeshev) | he shall dwell alone | Social isolation; LXX: μόνος κατοικήσει |
| טָמֵא טָמֵא (tameh tameh) | Unclean, unclean! | Doubled public cry; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος ἀκάθαρτος |
| שְׁתִי (sheti) | warp | Longitudinal loom threads; LXX: στήμων |
| עֵרֶב ('erev) | woof | Cross/transverse loom threads; LXX: κρόκη |
| יְרַקְרַק (yeraqraq) | greenish | Intensified form of yaroq; LXX: χλωρίζον |
| אֲדַמְדַּם (adamdam) | reddish | Intensified form of adom; LXX: πυρρίζον |
| צָרַעַת נוֹשֶׁנֶת (tzara'at noshenet) | chronic skin disease | Long-standing condition; LXX: λέπρα ἐνιαυσίζουσα |
| צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת (tzara'at mameret) | persistent skin disease | Recurring/malignant; LXX: λέπρα ἐντετηκυῖα |
| תּוֹרָה (torah) | law | Normative instruction; LXX: νόμος |
| בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (benei Yisrael) | sons of Israel | Formal address; consistent throughout Leviticus |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | The tabernacle (not invoked ch.13 but established) |

— LOGOS
